case.case_title: Steve Jobs / Chapter Eight: Xerox and Lisa: Graphical User Interfaces / tension_reversal
case.author: Walter Isaacson
case.chapter_title: Chapter Eight: Xerox and Lisa: Graphical User Interfaces
case.selection_reason: Selected because the passage contains a pressure point or reversal that should reward a proportionate, text-grounded move. Anchor line: For example, the Xerox mouse had three buttons, was complicated, cost $300 apiece, and didn’t roll around smoothly; a few days after his second Xerox PARC vi...
case.judge_focus: Does the mechanism stay with the reversal or tension instead of flattening it into generic summary?
case.excerpt_text:
Jobs and his engineers significantly improved the graphical interface ideas they saw at Xerox PARC, and then were able to implement them in ways that Xerox never could accomplish.
For example, the Xerox mouse had three buttons, was complicated, cost $300 apiece, and didn’t roll around smoothly; a few days after his second Xerox PARC visit, Jobs went to a local industrial design firm, IDEO, and told one of its founders, Dean Hovey, that he wanted a simple single-button model that cost $15, “and I want to be able to use it on Formica and my blue jeans.”
Hovey complied.
